does: This paper introduces Direct Token Optimization (DTO), a self-contained unlearning method for large language models that operates entirely within a single model without external resources. The approach identifies and categorizes tokens within sequences to unlearn into target tokens (capturing critical knowledge to forget) and non-target tokens, then directly optimizes token-level objectives to remove the influence of training data subsets. The method achieves unlearning by minimizing the model's ability to predict target tokens while preserving performance on retain sets, eliminating dependencies on auxiliary language models, retain datasets, or commercial services that prior approaches required.
connects: This work builds on the emerging machine unlearning literature that addresses privacy and content moderation in neural networks, extending recent advances in LLM-specific unlearning methods. Previous approaches like SISA training and gradient-based unlearning required auxiliary models or retain sets to maintain utility while forgetting. DTO responds to practical limitations of these methods by eliminating external dependencies, connecting to broader work on privacy-preserving machine learning and the challenge of selective forgetting demonstrated in earlier neural network unlearning studies while remaining grounded in LLM-specific constraints.
enables: This self-contained approach opens pathways for practical deployment of unlearning in resource-constrained settings and privacy-sensitive applications where using external models or datasets is infeasible. The token-level optimization framework could enable rapid model adaptation for content moderation and compliance without infrastructure overhead. Future work might extend DTO to handle more complex unlearning scenarios, combine it with other privacy techniques like differential privacy, or develop better metrics for verifying complete knowledge removal while maintaining broader model capabilities and utility across diverse downstream tasks.
